front: You need a fast, high-level cost or duration estimate with very limited detailed information available.
back: Use Analogous Estimating (top-down). It is quick and uses historical data from similar projects. Less accurate than bottom-up but useful early in planning.
tip: Analogous = fast but lower accuracy. Bottom-up = high accuracy but time-consuming.
source: PMBOK Guide – Estimate Activity Durations / Estimate Costs

front: You have a detailed WBS, resource requirements, and historical data per work package. Which estimating method is most accurate?
back: Bottom-up Estimating. It aggregates detailed estimates from the lowest level of the WBS. Most accurate but requires the most effort and information.
tip: Bottom-up is preferred when you have good decomposition and data. It is the most reliable for final estimates.

front: You want to identify the root causes of recurring defects or quality problems.
back: Use a Fishbone (Ishikawa) diagram or 5 Whys analysis. These tools help systematically explore cause-and-effect relationships.
tip: Fishbone is excellent for brainstorming causes. 5 Whys is good for drilling down to root cause quickly.

front: You need to prioritize problems or defects by frequency or impact to focus improvement efforts.
back: Use a Pareto Chart (80/20 rule). It helps you focus on the ‘vital few’ causes that create most of the problems.
tip: Pareto is a prioritization tool. Combine it with fishbone for analysis + prioritization.

front: You have many identified risks and limited time. You need to prioritize them quickly for further analysis.
back: Use Qualitative Risk Analysis (Probability × Impact matrix). It is fast and helps you focus on high-priority risks before doing quantitative analysis.
tip: Qualitative first for prioritization. Quantitative (EMV, Monte Carlo) for high-priority risks when more precision is needed.

figure.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 230 196' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><rect x='52' y='24' width='52' height='46' fill='#F5ECD8' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='0.8'/><rect x='104' y='24' width='52' height='46' fill='#F6E4DF' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='0.8'/><rect x='156' y='24' width='52' height='46' fill='#F6E4DF' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='0.8'/><rect x='52' y='70' width='52' height='46' fill='#E7F1ED' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='0.8'/><rect x='104' y='70' width='52' height='46' fill='#F5ECD8' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='0.8'/><rect x='156' y='70' width='52' height='46' fill='#F6E4DF' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='0.8'/><rect x='52' y='116' width='52' height='46' fill='#E7F1ED' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='0.8'/><rect x='104' y='116' width='52' height='46' fill='#E7F1ED' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='0.8'/><rect x='156' y='116' width='52' height='46' fill='#F5ECD8' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='0.8'/><text x='44' y='51' fill='currentColor' font-size='10' text-anchor='end'>H</text><text x='44' y='97' fill='currentColor' font-size='10' text-anchor='end'>M</text><text x='44' y='143' fill='currentColor' font-size='10' text-anchor='end'>L</text><text x='78' y='178' fill='currentColor' font-size='9' text-anchor='middle'>Low</text><text x='130' y='178' fill='currentColor' font-size='9' text-anchor='middle'>Med</text><text x='182' y='178' fill='currentColor' font-size='9' text-anchor='middle'>High</text><text transform='rotate(-90 14 93)' x='14' y='93' fill='currentColor' font-size='9' text-anchor='middle'>Probability</text><text x='130' y='192' fill='currentColor' font-size='9' text-anchor='middle'>Impact</text></svg>
figure.caption: Probability × impact: red cells (top-right) are your high-priority risks.

source: PMBOK Guide – Perform Qualitative Risk Analysis

front: You need to communicate complex or sensitive information where immediate feedback and clarification are important.
back: Use Interactive Communication (face-to-face meetings, video calls, workshops). It is the most effective for complex or emotional topics.
tip: Interactive > Pull > Push for important or complex messages. Choose the method based on the message and audience.

front: You need to quantify the potential financial impact of key risks and compare different response options.
back: Use Expected Monetary Value (EMV) analysis, often as part of a decision tree. Multiply probability by impact for each outcome and sum per option, then pick the highest EMV.
tip: EMV is excellent for comparing options in a decision tree. Remember: threats are negative, opportunities are positive.

figure.svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 330 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><rect x='18' y='92' width='16' height='16' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.4'/><text x='26' y='126' fill='currentColor' font-size='9' text-anchor='middle'>decision</text><line x1='34' y1='98' x2='119' y2='56' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.2'/><line x1='34' y1='102' x2='119' y2='146' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.2'/><circle cx='130' cy='54' r='11' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.3'/><text x='130' y='58' fill='currentColor' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle'>A</text><circle cx='130' cy='146' r='11' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.3'/><text x='130' y='150' fill='currentColor' font-size='11' text-anchor='middle'>B</text><line x1='141' y1='50' x2='236' y2='34' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1'/><text x='240' y='37' fill='currentColor' font-size='9'>+100 × 0.6</text><line x1='141' y1='58' x2='236' y2='76' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1'/><text x='240' y='79' fill='currentColor' font-size='9'>−20 × 0.4</text><line x1='141' y1='142' x2='236' y2='124' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1'/><text x='240' y='127' fill='currentColor' font-size='9'>+40 × 0.9</text><line x1='141' y1='150' x2='236' y2='168' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1'/><text x='240' y='171' fill='currentColor' font-size='9'>−10 × 0.1</text><text x='150' y='100' fill='#1F5FA8' font-size='10'>A = +52</text><text x='150' y='116' fill='#2F6F5E' font-size='10'>B = +35</text></svg>
figure.caption: Each branch = probability × impact; sum per option, choose the higher EMV (A).

source: PMBOK Guide – Perform Quantitative Risk Analysis

front: Requirements are unclear, likely to change, and stakeholder needs are evolving rapidly.
back: Use Agile/Iterative approaches with user stories, backlog prioritization, and frequent feedback loops. Avoid heavy upfront detailed requirements documentation.
tip: When uncertainty is high, favor adaptive approaches over predictive detailed planning.
